candidates.title: Meta's New Glasses Could Make Zuckerberg A Trillionaire
candidates.description: Meta just dropped Ray-Ban from its smart glasses entirely. After selling at least 9 million Ray-Ban Meta pairs, Zuckerberg unveiled a trio of his own AI frames — the Fury, the Adventurer, and a Kylie Jenner-designed edition — starting at $299, about $80 cheaper than last year's Ray-Ban model and roughly 90% less than Snap's $2,000 face computer. But the real story isn't the price. Meta is treating smart glasses like a car company, with a model for every tier, plans to strip out the cameras for a sub-$100 audio pair, and even facial recognition that tells you who you're looking at. We break down why these frames are really Zuckerberg's bid to replace the iPhone — and become the world's second

candidates.title: Google's $75M A24 Deal Could Reshape Every Movie
candidates.description: Google just took a $75 million equity stake in A24 — its first-ever investment in a film studio — and tied it to a multi-year AI research partnership with DeepMind. While most of Hollywood is busy suing AI companies for copyright infringement, the anti-blockbuster studio behind Everything Everywhere All at Once is doing the exact opposite. The DeepMind tools are aimed at reshoots, editing, and VFX rather than generating films outright. We get into why Google is double-dipping on the future of Hollywood — it already owns YouTube — and whether this deal is a genuine creative breakthrough or something we're calling "taste-washing." Then, Grillo's Pickles added 100,000 followers in a single week

candidates.title: One Rule Beats Every Amazon Prime Day Deal
candidates.description: A new lawsuit claims Amazon's Prime Day is "rife with fake sales" — and this year the event runs a full four days. We dug into the pricing playbook nearly every major retailer leans on, from phantom "regular" prices that were never real to the countdown timers engineered to make you skip the math. Then we lay out how to call the bluff: the free price-history tool that exposes a fake discount in seconds, the chatbot trick that quietly talks the price down, and why this could be the last Prime Day your emotions can be used against you. Then: why did a private equity giant buy a legendary Napa Valley spa out of foreclosure? Blackstone picked up the 135-room Stanly Ranch resort after its previou

candidates.title: Ben & Jerry's Founder On Being Silenced
candidates.description: We sit down with Ben Cohen, the co-founder of Ben & Jerry's, who is now fighting to buy back the company that still carries his name. Back in 2000, he and Jerry Greenfield sold Ben & Jerry's to Unilever for $326 million — but structured one of the most unusual deals in corporate history to protect the brand's social mission. Now Ben says the corporate owners have muzzled the brand he built, his best friend and co-founder Jerry has resigned, and Ben has launched a campaign to free Ben & Jerry's. We ask him whether mixing politics and business is actually profitable, what Ben & Jerry's 2.0 would look like, and how far he'll go to win it back. Then Ben takes us back to the beginning — how two b

candidates.title: How SpaceX Turned Into Elon's $2T ATM
candidates.description: SpaceX IPO'd on Friday, and within one trading day it became the first mega meme stock in history. Elon Musk sold $86 billion in shares — 90% of it to Wall Street — and then retail investors did something almost no one predicted: by Monday, 74% of every single-stock purchase they made was SpaceX, sending the stock up 50%. Here's the twist. With shares soaring and only 4% of the company actually available to trade, Musk immediately started selling. We break down what he did with the money in just three business days, and the one advantage he has that no other CEO on earth can copy. Toy Story 5 hits theaters this weekend, tracking for a $175 million domestic opening — the biggest debut of 2026

candidates.description: A new lawsuit claims Amazon's Prime Day is "rife with fake sales" — and this year the event runs a full four days. candidates.title: Fox's $22B Roku Deal Could Hit Your Portfolio
candidates.description: Roku started as a $50 streaming box plugged into the back of your TV — and 18 years later, Fox agreed to buy it for $22 billion. The deal values Roku at $160 a share and hands Fox the #1 connected-TV platform in America, where 1 in 4 living rooms already stream through Roku. But Fox stock dropped roughly 20% on the news, because the company many expected Google, Meta, or Walmart to buy is now owned by a legacy cable giant. We break down why a "dying" cable company spent $22 billion on the box that helped kill cable. Then, Hyrox didn't exist a decade ago. Now it's the fastest-growing sport in the world — 1.5 million paid participants across 16,000 affiliate gyms, $270 million in revenue, and

candidates.title: Waymo's New $30 Subscription Isn't About Rides
candidates.description: Waymo's first-ever subscription is here: Waymo Premier, an invite-only $30-a-month membership that costs more than Netflix and three times Uber One. Members get priority pickups, 10% cash back in Waymo Cash, and free cancellations — all while the robotaxi is banned in NYC and Boston, launching its first national TV ads, and hitting 500,000 rides a week. We ran the money math on exactly how many rides make Premier pay for itself, and why Waymo's future looks less like a taxi and more like a credit card. Then, Bernie Sanders and Donald Trump agree on something: the US government owning a piece of the AI companies. Sanders' American AI Sovereign Wealth Fund Act would collect a one-time 50% tax
